evidence_label: Highly uncertain — plausible range ≈75,000–200,000
event_label: Canaanite city-states under Egyptian rule
scenario_note: All residents are combined in one visualization category. The selected total is 150,000 and the adopted runtime upper is 200,000; the broader 250,000 scenario remains an outer research sensitivity. Because all residents are combined at this anchor, the Other / Unknown plausible upper equals the total-population upper. Equality with the 1200 BCE selected total does not imply measured demographic stasis.
demographic_context_label: Around 1500 BCE, residents lived in towns, farming villages and mobile herding communities. Later identities cannot yet be distinguished reliably, so all are shown together.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Broshi & Gophna; Bunimovitz; Finkelstein; Kennedy — project Late Bronze synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — reconstructed from archaeology; plausible total ≈100,000–200,000
event_label: Bronze Age systems contract; coastal and highland societies emerge
lineage_note: The simultaneous forks represent emerging highland and coastal societies becoming analytically distinguishable. Philistine society formed through interaction between local populations and migrants; the branches are not literal ancestry transfers or evidence of population replacement.
scenario_note: The selected total is 150,000 and the separately stored total-population plausible upper is 200,000. Proto-Israelites are selected at 17,500 and Philistines at 25,000, leaving 107,500 Other / Unknown. The conditional Other / Unknown plausible upper is 157,500 (= 200,000 total upper − 17,500 selected Proto-Israelites − 25,000 selected Philistines). Community plausible uppers are independent and must not be summed into the total. The simultaneous forks represent analytical distinguishability, not sudden replacement.
demographic_context_label: Older Canaanite communities continue as Bronze Age systems contract. Philistine society forms from local people and migrants on the southern coast, while new highland villages expand.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Broshi & Finkelstein; Yasur-Landau — project archaeological-demographic synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — settlement-based scenario; plausible total ≈120,000–200,000
event_label: Early Israelite monarchy; extent debated
lineage_note: The Israelites bar remains combined until the c.850 BCE political-regional fork.
scenario_note: The 65,000 Israelite value and 180,000 total are retained. A 30,000 Philistine regional allocation is separated from the residual, leaving 85,000 Other / Unknown. The conditional Other / Unknown plausible upper is 105,000 (= 200,000 total upper − 65,000 selected Israelites − 30,000 selected Philistines). Community plausible uppers are independent and must not be summed into the total. The residual is not a measured population or a claim of demographic collapse.
demographic_context_label: An early Israelite monarchy is traditionally placed around this time, though its scale is debated. Philistine cities remain major coastal powers within a wider landscape of local communities.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Broshi & Finkelstein; Yasur-Landau; state-formation studies — project synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — interpolated archaeological scenario; plausible total ≈220,000–360,000
era_label: Kingdom of Israel / Kingdom of Judah
event_label: The Kingdom of Israel and the Kingdom of Judah emerge clearly
lineage_note: The fork represents two ninth-century political-regional systems becoming clearer in the evidence, not a sudden ethnic division or population replacement.
scenario_note: Project interpolation retains the 300,000 total: 40,000 Judeans, 120,000 Israelites associated with the Kingdom of Israel, 40,000 Philistines and 100,000 Other / Unknown. The conditional Other / Unknown plausible upper is 160,000 (= 360,000 total upper − 40,000 selected Judeans − 120,000 selected Israelites − 40,000 selected Philistines). Community plausible uppers are independent and must not be summed into the total. The allocations are rounded archaeological scenarios, not census counts.
demographic_context_label: The Kingdom of Israel and the Kingdom of Judah are now clearly attested. Philistine city-states and other coastal and lowland communities remain important parts of the region.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Broshi & Finkelstein; Tel Dan and state-formation studies; Philistine regional studies — project synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — reconstructed from archaeology and historical evidence
event_label: Assyria conquers the Kingdom of Israel; deportations follow
lineage_note: The northern political category remains visible at the anchor and ends only in the following transition; the fall of the state must not be rendered as disappearance of its inhabitants.
scenario_note: Eve-of-conquest scenario. The existing 240,000 combined allocation to Judeans and Israelites of the Kingdom of Israel remains unchanged; 50,000 Philistines are separated from the residual, leaving 110,000 Other / Unknown. The political-population split is not an ethnic census.
demographic_context_label: These bars show residents on the eve of the Kingdom of Israel’s fall. Deportation, local survival and resettlement reshape the region under Assyrian rule.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Broshi & Finkelstein; Assyrian-continuity and Philistine regional studies — project synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — reconstructed from archaeology and historical evidence
event_label: Assyrian king Sennacherib invades the Kingdom of Judah; its capital, Jerusalem, survives
lineage_note: The Judean continuity bar may include incorporated northern-origin residents; the northern/local composite includes local survivors, incoming settlers, coastal groups and unresolved classifications.
scenario_note: Selected values remain 220,000 Judeans, with 50,000 Philistines separated from the residual and 90,000 Other / Unknown. The former Kingdom of Israel lineage merges qualitatively into both continuing bars; no transfer percentage or census-like survivor count is asserted.
demographic_context_label: After the Kingdom of Israel’s fall, some Israelites remain or move into Judah; others are deported. Sennacherib devastates Judah and Philistine cities, but Jerusalem survives.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Broshi & Finkelstein; Assyrian migration/continuity and Philistine studies — project synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — reconstructed from archaeology and historical evidence
event_label: Babylon destroys Jerusalem and ends the Kingdom of Judah
lineage_note: The Philistine political-cultural category merges qualitatively into Other / Unknown during the c.604–586 BCE shock window. This ends the chart category, not every Philistine-origin lineage; no transfer percentage or c.604 population count is asserted.
scenario_note: Selected values remain 70,000 Judeans and 130,000 Other / Unknown at 586 BCE. The c.604 start year controls interpolation only: all displayed populations remain at their 701 BCE values until then, then move toward the 586 BCE endpoints. Philistines equal zero because the separate category ends; zero is not a survivor count.
demographic_context_label: Babylon breaks the Philistine city-state system around 604 BCE, then destroys Jerusalem and ends the Kingdom of Judah in 586 BCE. Many residents are killed, displaced or deported, while others survive.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Yehud/Samaria; northern-continuity; Philistine-disappearance studies — project synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — reconstructed from archaeology and historical evidence
event_label: Cyrus the Great’s Persian Empire replaces Babylonian rule
scenario_note: Selected values remain 35,000 Judeans and 135,000 Other / Unknown. The 170,000 residual upper is an arithmetic scenario, not an estimate for one community.
demographic_context_label: The region includes the Persian provinces of Yehud and Samaria, alongside Idumaea and Phoenician-linked coastal districts. Some Judean exiles return over generations.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Yehud/Samaria; northern-continuity; Philistine-disappearance studies — project synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — reconstructed from archaeology and historical evidence
lineage_note: The fork represents improved analytical distinguishability of a Samaritan community; it does not claim that all northern survivors became Samaritans or that identity changed in one year.
scenario_note: Selected values remain 35,000 Judeans, 60,000 Samaritans and 85,000 Other / Unknown. The 105,000 residual upper is an arithmetic scenario, not an estimate for one community.
demographic_context_label: A Gerizim- and Samaria-centred Israelite community can now be shown separately. It is one part of a wider mosaic of Judeans, Idumaeans, Phoenician-linked coastal communities and southern groups.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Yehud/Samaria; Samaritan; northern-continuity and Achaemenid coastal studies — project synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — reconstructed from archaeology and historical evidence

territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — regional archaeological synthesis; plausible total ≈180,000–410,000

scenario_note: S23 fixed-territory synthesis. The 90,000 Jewish and 75,000 Samaritan selected values are retained; Hellenistic resident communities rise to 15,000 and Other / Unknown to 100,000 so non-Judean parts of the fixed territory are not compressed into an implausibly small residual.

data_source_label: Finkelstein; Leibner; Levin; Bourgel; Dąbrowa — project Hasmonean synthesis

evidence_label: Highly uncertain — regional archaeological synthesis; plausible total ≈240,000–620,000

The increase reflects natural growth, migration, settlement, partial incorporation or conversion and changing historical visibility; conquest alone does not add residents to the fixed-territory total.
demographic_context_label: Jewish settlement and identity spread through migration, local incorporation and sometimes coercion or displacement. Conquered populations did not become Jewish all at once.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Finkelstein; Leibner; Levin; Bourgel; Dąbrowa — project Hasmonean synthesis

Pompey's intervention changes rule and borders after the anchor; it does not cause the preceding population growth or imply an instantaneous Roman resident influx.
demographic_context_label: The region’s population had grown under the Hasmoneans—especially its Jewish population—before Pompey’s conquest brought Roman rule.
territory_scope: Modern Israel excluding the Golan Heights + West Bank including East Jerusalem + Gaza Strip
data_source_label: Finkelstein; Leibner; Levin; Bourgel; Dąbrowa — project Hasmonean synthesis
